title: Set up a reproducible benchmark-and-analysis study
description: Prepare a lean PyTorch environment for a single-GPU, 8-hour study focused on the narrow question: when low-rank span representatives help over point representatives under matched supervised contrastive training.

steps.step1: Create a fresh Python 3.10+ environment and install `torch`, `torchvision`, `numpy`, `scipy`, `scikit-learn`, `pandas`, `matplotlib`, `seaborn`, `tqdm`, and `pyyaml` with one CUDA-enabled PyTorch build matched to the local NVIDIA driver.
steps.step2: Record `python --version`, `pip freeze`, `nvidia-smi`, `free -h`, and `nproc` once in `results/system_info.txt` so runtime and hardware claims are auditable.
steps.step3: Use a fixed output layout: `data/` for datasets, `configs/` for frozen configs, `exp/<method>/<setting>/<seed>/` for logs and checkpoints, `results/` for merged CSV/JSON summaries, and `figures/` for paper-ready plots.
steps.step4: Fix the acceptance-critical seed list to `[11, 22, 33]` and use the same seed value across matched method comparisons. Use no extra seed sweep beyond these three.
steps.step5: Enable automatic mixed precision, batch accumulation disabled, `num_workers=4`, `pin_memory=true`, `persistent_workers=true`, and deterministic validation. Log wall-clock time, peak GPU memory, and parameter count for every run.
steps.step6: Run a 3-epoch smoke test for `SupCon`, `PSC`, `MPSC`, `CLOP-style`, and `Span` on CIFAR-100 coarse labels and for the synthetic pipeline before any full run. Verify decreasing loss, finite normalized embeddings, valid all-class logits, and successful evaluation-script completion.
steps.step7: Treat the machine as one-GPU only for training. Do not claim GPU parallelism. Only overlap CPU-side linear-probe fitting, k-NN evaluation, summary aggregation, and plotting with ongoing GPU training.

title: Prepare CIFAR-100 and a two-regime synthetic benchmark
description: Use one primary real benchmark and one synthetic benchmark tailored to the theory claim. Keep data handling identical across methods so the comparison isolates representative family choice.

steps.step1: Download CIFAR-100 into `data/cifar100/` via torchvision and verify the standard split sizes of 50,000 training and 10,000 test images.
steps.step2: Save the official 100-fine to 20-coarse label mapping in `results/cifar100_label_map.json`. Use coarse labels for pretraining and fine labels only for evaluation and the limited sanity check.
steps.step3: Create a stratified validation split by holding out 5,000 images from the original training set, stratified by fine label, and save indices in `results/cifar100_splits.json`. Use the same split for every method.
steps.step4: For all contrastive methods, use exactly two augmented views per image with `RandomResizedCrop(32, scale=(0.6, 1.0))`, `RandomHorizontalFlip(0.5)`, `ColorJitter(0.4, 0.4, 0.4, 0.1)` with probability `0.8`, `RandomGrayscale(0.2)`, and CIFAR-100 mean/std normalization. For linear probes, use only normalization.
steps.step5: Build a synthetic normalized-embedding benchmark in `data/synthetic_modes/` with `C=10` classes, `M=2` latent subclasses per class, feature dimension `d=32`, true latent rank `r_true=2`, and 600 train plus 200 test samples per class.
steps.step6: Generate two mandatory synthetic regimes using the proposal's latent-mode model before normalization, then normalize every sample: `directional_low_rank` with small subclass mean gaps and strong low-rank covariance energy, and `mean_or_isotropic` with larger subclass mean gaps and nearly isotropic residual variation. Keep class count, sample count, and noise scale otherwise fixed.
steps.step7: Store ground-truth class labels, subclass labels, subclass means, and true basis matrices so evaluation can compute adjusted mutual information, subclass recovery accuracy, and principal-angle error between learned and true subspaces.

title: Run matched point-based and class-control baselines
description: Use one simple baseline and multiple strong baselines, all under the same backbone, optimizer, temperatures, and evaluation pipeline. The goal is a fair benchmark, not a broad method zoo.

steps.step1: Use the same encoder for all real-data representation-learning runs: `ResNet-18` with a two-layer projection head `512 -> 512 -> 128`, L2-normalized 128-dimensional embeddings, SGD with momentum `0.9`, weight decay `1e-4`, cosine learning-rate decay, base learning rate `0.5`, batch size `256`, and `30` epochs.
steps.step2: Train one simple baseline, standard `SupCon`, on CIFAR-100 coarse labels for seeds `11,22,33` with temperature `0.1`. This is the minimum point of comparison for subclass preservation under standard supervised contrastive learning.
steps.step3: Train `PSC` as one persistent point representative per class under the same schedule. Compute class scores for all classes as `ell_y(z)=tau_c*logsumexp_k(s_{y,k}(z)/tau_c)` with `K=1`, `s_{y,1}(z)=z^T p_y`, and `tau_c=0.2`. Run seeds `11,22,33`.
steps.step4: Train `MPSC` as the strong direct point-family comparator with `K=2` point representatives per class. Use `s_{y,k}(z)=z^T p_{y,k}`, assignment probabilities `q_{i,k}=softmax_k(s_{y_i,k}(z_i)/tau_a)` with `tau_a=0.1`, and same-label pair weights `w_ij=q_i^T q_j`. Run seeds `11,22,33`.
steps.step5: Train a `CLOP-style control` baseline as a class-level anti-collapse control, not a within-class multi-mode model. Use either one orthonormal prototype or one rank-2 class subspace per coarse class inside the same classification-plus-SupCon template, with seeds `11,22,33`.
steps.step6: For every baseline, keep negatives identical to standard SupCon: all samples from different coarse labels remain negatives. Do not add class-balancing, pseudo-subclass clustering, resets, or auxiliary autoencoders.
steps.step7: Save per-run outputs as `results.json` with training curves, validation metrics, final test metrics, runtime, memory, and the exact hyperparameter values so the benchmark can be reproduced directly.

title: Train the low-rank span representative family
description: Evaluate the span model strictly as a matched alternative to point representatives. The loss and scoring rules must be defined for all classes so the comparison is theoretically and empirically clean.

steps.step1: Use the identical real-data training recipe as the baselines: same `ResNet-18`, same 128-dimensional normalized embedding, same optimizer, same batch size, same `30`-epoch schedule, and same augmentations.
steps.step2: For each coarse class `y`, maintain `K=2` span representatives with orthonormal bases `U_{y,k}` of shape `128 x 2`. Define span scores for every class and every representative as `s_{y,k}(z)=z^T U_{y,k}U_{y,k}^T z = ||U_{y,k}^T z||_2^2`.
steps.step3: Compute all-class logits for every sample using `ell_y(z)=tau_c*logsumexp_k(s_{y,k}(z)/tau_c)` for all `y` with `tau_c=0.2`, then class probabilities `pi_y(z)=exp(ell_y(z))/sum_{y'} exp(ell_{y'}(z))`. Use these logits for the classification term on both positive and negative classes, not only the ground-truth class.
steps.step4: Use the same weighted-SupCon wrapper as `MPSC`: `q_{i,k}=softmax_k(s_{y_i,k}(z_i)/tau_a)` with `tau_a=0.1`, `w_ij=q_i^T q_j` for same-label pairs, and standard different-label negatives. Optimize `L = L_ce + 1.0 * L_wsupcon + 0.05 * L_div + 0.02 * L_cov`.
steps.step5: Define `L_div` as within-class span overlap penalty `sum_y sum_{k<k'} ||U_{y,k}^T U_{y,k'}||_F^2` and `L_cov` as a mild occupancy entropy penalty that discourages permanently dormant spans without forcing balanced assignments.
steps.step6: After each optimizer step, re-orthonormalize each `U_{y,k}` with QR decomposition. Log assignment entropy, dormant-span fraction, and within-class span overlap each epoch. Do not use prototype/span resets or occupancy forcing in the default method.
steps.step7: Run the full span method on CIFAR-100 coarse labels for seeds `11,22,33`, then evaluate with the same frozen linear probe and `20`-NN procedure used for all baselines.

title: Validate the theory prediction and ablate span-specific components
description: Keep analysis tight: one synthetic theory validation and three real-data ablations, each directly tied to a span-specific component. Avoid extra settings that do not change the publishable conclusion.

steps.step1: Synthetic theory validation: train `SupCon`, `PSC`, `MPSC`, and `Span` on both synthetic regimes with a small MLP encoder `32 -> 128 -> 32`, Adam learning rate `1e-3`, batch size `512`, and `15` epochs. Run seeds `11,22,33` for every method-regime pair.
steps.step2: Measure synthetic adjusted mutual information, subclass recovery accuracy, and principal-angle error. The intended falsifiable pattern is: `Span > MPSC` in `directional_low_rank`, and the advantage shrinks materially or vanishes in `mean_or_isotropic`.
steps.step3: Ablation A, point-limit test: rerun the span method on CIFAR-100 coarse labels with span rank reduced from `2` to `1` while keeping `K=2` and all other settings fixed. Run seeds `11,22,33`. This tests whether gains require nontrivial low-rank spans rather than multiple representatives alone.
steps.step4: Ablation B, no diversity penalty: set `L_div=0` and rerun the coarse-to-fine benchmark for seeds `11,22,33`. This tests whether the default result depends on explicit anti-duplication of spans.
steps.step5: Ablation C, no coverage penalty: set `L_cov=0` and rerun the coarse-to-fine benchmark for seeds `11,22,33`. This tests whether the mild occupancy encouragement is necessary.
steps.step6: Do not run extra ablations on `K`, extra synthetic settings, fine-label full-matrix comparisons, or reset mechanisms. Those are intentionally excluded to preserve the 8-hour compute story.
steps.step7: If any ablation exceeds the runtime budget in practice, keep the three-seed rank-1 ablation first, then three-seed no-`L_div`, then three-seed no-`L_cov`. Do not cut the synthetic theory validation or the main coarse-to-fine baseline matrix.

title: Evaluate coarse-to-fine transfer, geometry, and failure cases
description: Use one primary real benchmark and one limited sanity check. Report outcomes that can confirm or disprove the narrow benchmark claim without overstating novelty.

steps.step1: Primary real benchmark: pretrain on CIFAR-100 coarse labels, freeze the encoder, then evaluate on fine labels with a `30`-epoch linear probe and a `20`-nearest-neighbor classifier. Report test top-1 accuracy and macro-F1 for `SupCon`, `PSC`, `MPSC`, `CLOP-style`, `Span`, and the three span ablations.
steps.step2: Training-quality safeguard: for `SupCon`, `MPSC`, and `Span` only, run a one-seed fine-label training sanity check on seed `11` using the same `ResNet-18` recipe and report fine-label top-1 accuracy. Use this only to ensure the span method does not catastrophically hurt ordinary discriminative performance.
steps.step3: Geometry diagnostics on real data: compute within-class effective rank over fine labels after coarse-label pretraining, average same-class cosine spread, average span overlap, and dormant-span fraction. Report these alongside transfer metrics, not in place of them.
steps.step4: Aggregate all acceptance-critical results as mean and standard deviation over seeds `11,22,33`. Also report paired per-seed differences of `Span` versus `SupCon`, `PSC`, and `MPSC` on coarse-to-fine linear-probe accuracy.
steps.step5: Success criteria: support the claim only if `Span` improves over `SupCon` by at least `0.5` points on CIFAR-100 coarse-to-fine linear-probe or `20`-NN accuracy, is not worse than the better of `PSC` and `MPSC` by more than `0.3` points on the primary transfer metric, and beats `MPSC` on at least one synthetic recovery metric in `directional_low_rank` while the advantage shrinks in `mean_or_isotropic`.
steps.step6: Failure criteria: mark the claim as weakened if `Span` beats `SupCon` but not `MPSC`, or if synthetic regime differences are absent. Mark it as refuted if `MPSC` matches or beats `Span` on both synthetic `directional_low_rank` recovery and coarse-to-fine transfer.
steps.step7: Interpret `CLOP-style` results only as a control on whether class-level anti-collapse already explains the gains. If it matches or beats both `MPSC` and `Span`, conclude that the experiment does not isolate a useful within-class representative-family advantage in this compute regime.

title: Produce figures and tables for the benchmark claim
description: Generate only the artifacts needed to support or reject the point-versus-span claim. Keep the presentation centered on matched comparisons and synthetic regime dependence.

steps.step1: Create one main real-data table with rows `SupCon`, `PSC`, `MPSC`, `CLOP-style`, and `Span`, and columns coarse-to-fine linear-probe top-1, coarse-to-fine `20`-NN top-1, macro-F1, runtime, and peak GPU memory.
steps.step2: Create one synthetic-results table with rows `SupCon`, `PSC`, `MPSC`, and `Span`, and columns AMI, subclass recovery accuracy, and principal-angle error for `directional_low_rank` and `mean_or_isotropic`.
steps.step3: Create one span-ablation table with rows full `Span`, rank-1, no `L_div`, and no `L_cov`, and columns coarse-to-fine linear-probe top-1, `20`-NN top-1, dormant-span fraction, overlap penalty value, runtime, and peak memory.
steps.step4: Plot span-minus-MPSC synthetic performance as grouped bars over the two regimes for AMI and principal-angle error so the theory prediction is visually explicit.
steps.step5: Plot coarse-to-fine linear-probe accuracy with seed-level dots for `SupCon`, `PSC`, `MPSC`, `CLOP-style`, and `Span` so mean differences and variance are both visible.
steps.step6: Plot span diagnostics across epochs for one representative run: assignment entropy, dormant-span fraction, and average within-class span overlap.
steps.step7: Save all figures as both PNG and PDF under `figures/`, and save the underlying numeric plotting data as CSV under `results/figure_data/`.

title: Fit the study within one A6000 and eight hours
description: Use conservative runtime estimates and a strict priority order. The plan assumes one GPU, 4 CPU cores, and no hidden extra hardware.

steps.step1: Use these conservative per-run estimates on 1x RTX A6000 with AMP and CIFAR-sized inputs: `SupCon` coarse-label run `10-12` minutes, `PSC` `10-12` minutes, `MPSC` `11-13` minutes, `CLOP-style` `10-12` minutes, `Span` `12-14` minutes, fine-label sanity run `10-12` minutes, and each synthetic run `1-2` minutes including evaluation.
steps.step2: Count the core real-data matrix as `15` runs: `5` methods times `3` seeds on the primary coarse-to-fine benchmark. Estimated wall-clock is about `3.0-3.4` hours.
steps.step3: Count the synthetic theory block as `24` runs: `4` methods times `2` regimes times `3` seeds. Estimated wall-clock is about `0.4-0.8` hours.
steps.step4: Count the span ablations as `9` runs: `3` ablations times `3` seeds on CIFAR-100 coarse labels. Estimated wall-clock is about `1.8-2.1` hours.
steps.step5: Count the limited fine-label safeguard as `3` runs: `SupCon`, `MPSC`, and `Span` on seed `11` only. Estimated wall-clock is about `0.5-0.6` hours.
steps.step6: Reserve about `0.5-0.8` hours total for smoke tests, checkpoint verification, linear-probe fitting, k-NN evaluation, metrics aggregation, and figure generation. Expected total wall-clock is therefore about `6.2-7.7` hours.
steps.step7: Run jobs in this order: smoke tests, full coarse-to-fine core matrix, synthetic theory block, span ablations, then the one-seed fine-label safeguard. This ordering ensures the benchmark claim is fully testable even if the lowest-priority sanity runs are skipped late.
steps.step8: If elapsed time exceeds `7.25` hours before the final block, keep all completed coarse-to-fine runs, all synthetic runs, and the rank-1 ablation first. Drop the one-seed fine-label safeguard before dropping any three-seed acceptance-critical comparison.
